brief:
The single most striking feature of this week's curve is not what moved but what didn't — **zero basis points of change across every tenor**, from 91-day bills to the 20-year bond. This kind of total stillness is not calm; it is a market holding its breath. When yields freeze across the entire curve despite ৳1.9 lakh crore of excess liquidity sloshing through the system and inflation still running at **9.13%**, the message is clear: participants are paralyzed between two competing narratives. On one hand, the short end at **9.85%** for 91-day bills sits comfortably below the **10% repo rate**, which tells you the market believes the next policy move is a cut, or at the very least that BB has no appetite to tighten further. On the other hand, the long end refuses to rally — the 15-year at **12.68%** and the 20-year at **11.65%** reflect deep-seated fiscal anxiety that no one is willing to price away. And here lies an anomaly that demands your attention: the **2-year bond at 11.95%** is trading above the 5-year at **10.82%**, the 10-year at **11.15%**, and even the 20-year at **11.65%**. That is not a normal curve; that is a knot of distress at the 2-year point, almost certainly reflecting a supply overhang or a specific issuance that the market could not digest cleanly. The 10-year to 91-day spread of **130 basis points** looks deceptively modest for a curve that carries this much internal dislocation.

The structural backdrop explains why liquidity abundance has not translated into yield compression. Revenue collection at **44.9% of the FY26 target** with barely four months left in the fiscal year means the government is staring at a yawning gap that will have to be filled by borrowing — and everyone in this room knows what happens to bond supply after Eid and into the June crunch. ADP implementation at a paltry **11.5%** is actually keeping a lid on things for now, because the spending hasn't hit the economy yet; when it does, it will drain some of that ৳1.9 lakh crore surplus and simultaneously add to inflationary pressure that is already uncomfortably high. The ADR at **75.8%** suggests banks have ample room to deploy into government paper, and the call money rate at **9.8%** — twenty basis points below repo — confirms that interbank funding is easy. But here is the critical tension: with CPI at **9.13%** and food inflation at **9.3%**, real returns on the short end are essentially zero. The SDF at **7.5%** is deeply negative in real terms, which means the ৳1.9 lakh crore parked in the system is earning returns that inflation is eating alive. M2 growth at **9.5%** is running just above inflation, which means real money supply growth is negligible — hardly the kind of environment that supports aggressive duration extension. Meanwhile, Brent at **$78.4** and JKM LNG at **$12.8/mmbtu** are not alarming, but they keep imported inflation risk firmly on the table, particularly with the taka at **121.2** against the dollar and reserves at **$35.1 billion**, which while adequate, leave limited room for aggressive FX defense if commodity prices spike.

The actionable call this week centers on exploiting the grotesque 2-year anomaly and positioning for the inevitable fiscal year-end supply wave. The **2-year at 11.95%** is offering **113 basis points over the 5-year** — that is a gift if the dislocation corrects, and it represents extraordinary carry even if it doesn't. I would be adding 2-year paper aggressively at these levels; the risk-reward is as asymmetric as it gets on the Bangladeshi curve right now. Conversely, I would be cautious on the **10-year at 11.15%** where the last auction's bid-cover of **1.4x with 10% devolvement** tells you the market is barely willing to absorb supply at current yields. If the government accelerates long-end issuance to plug the revenue shortfall, 10-year yields have room to back up by 15-25 basis points. The short-end bills — 91-day at **1.92x bid-cover** and 364-day at **1.75x** with zero devolvement — are well-bid and liquid, making them ideal parking spots for any excess SLR holdings. The catalyst to watch over the next two weeks is BB's April borrowing calendar and any signals on the policy corridor ahead of the next MPC: if BB signals even a modest repo cut to support growth, the front end will rally hard and that frozen curve will finally crack open — and we want to be positioned before it does, not after.
